title: Model Retraining Strategies
concept: Retraining strategies define how and when a stale model gets refreshed with new data — on a fixed schedule, triggered by drift alerts, or continuously updated.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is a proxy signal used for in model monitoring?

options.text: Giving an early warning of drift before ground truth is confirmed.
Compressing the model file for storage.

exercises.explain: Correct! Proxy signals give early hints before we have confirmed outcomes.

cards.title: Restocking a salad bar
cards.body: A restaurant has three ways to keep its salad bar fresh: 1) restock every morning at 8am regardless of what's left (scheduled), 2) restock only when a staff member notices something looks wilted (triggered), or 3) use a machine that continuously tops off ingredients all day (continuous). Model retraining works the same way.

cards.title: Three Retraining Strategies
cards.body: Scheduled retraining retrains on a fixed calendar (e.g. every Sunday). Triggered retraining retrains only when a monitoring alert fires (e.g. drift detected). Continuous retraining constantly updates the model as fresh data streams in.

cards.title: Oil changes vs. warning lights
cards.body: Scheduled retraining is like changing your car's oil every 5,000 miles regardless of condition — predictable but sometimes wasteful. Triggered retraining is like waiting for the check-engine light to flash — efficient but risks catching problems late.

cards.title: Choosing a strategy
cards.body: A weather forecasting model might use scheduled retraining (daily, since weather patterns shift predictably). A fraud detection model might use triggered retraining (retrain immediately when drift alerts fire, since fraud tactics can change suddenly and unpredictably).

cards.title: More frequent isn't always better
cards.body: You might assume continuous retraining is always the best choice since it's the freshest. In reality, retraining constantly is expensive (computing costs) and risky (a single batch of bad or malicious data can corrupt the model quickly). Teams pick the strategy that matches how fast their specific domain actually changes.

left.text:
Predictable, fixed calendar.
Simple to manage.
Can be wasteful or too slow.

right.text:
Reacts to drift alerts.
Efficient use of resources.
Depends on monitoring quality.

options.text: Retraining a model on a fixed, predictable calendar interval regardless of detected drift.
Retraining only when a human manually deletes the old model.
Retraining that never happens after initial deployment.
A method for compressing model files.

exercises.explain: Correct! Scheduled retraining runs on a fixed calendar, independent of drift signals.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Continuous retraining is always risk-free and should be used for every single model.
exercises.explain: False! Continuous retraining is expensive and risks corrupting the model quickly if bad data streams in.

exercises.sentence: ___ retraining only kicks off when a monitoring system detects a drift alert.
exercises.explain: Triggered retraining reacts specifically to drift alerts, rather than a fixed calendar.

exercises.prompt: A fraud detection team notices monitoring alerts firing unpredictably whenever fraudsters invent new tactics. Which retraining strategy fits best?

options.text: Triggered retraining, since fraud patterns change unpredictably and reacting immediately to alerts is more efficient than a fixed schedule.
Scheduled retraining once a year, since fraud rarely changes.
No retraining at all, since the original model is permanent.

exercises.explain: Correct! Since fraud shifts unpredictably, triggering retraining off real alerts is more responsive than a fixed calendar.

exercises.prompt: A retail company's demand forecasting model needs updates as seasons predictably change (winter to summer), while their fraud detection model needs updates whenever criminals invent new unpredictable tactics. Which strategies best fit each model?

options.text: Scheduled retraining for demand forecasting (predictable seasonal shifts) and triggered retraining for fraud detection (unpredictable, alert-driven shifts).
Triggered retraining for both models, since it is always superior.
Scheduled retraining for both models, since predictability is always more important.
Neither model should ever be retrained after initial deployment.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Predictable seasonal patterns fit a fixed schedule well, while unpredictable fraud tactics are better served by alert-driven, triggered retraining. Next, let's zoom out and look at the full engineering discipline that manages this entire lifecycle!
